When I do scroll through, I see so much questionable stuff that it makes me anxious about what information is actually real anymore. As an electrician, I work with systems that either function correctly or they don't. There's no middle ground when you're dealing with electrical codes and safety standards. I wish people approached information online with that same kind of precision, but they don't.

What bothers me most is how fake content affects my kid. I'm raising a child in this world where lies spread faster than truth, and that's genuinely concerning. I've started being extremely selective about what I engage with online. If something seems too outrageous or perfectly fits some narrative, I just skip it entirely. I don't have time to fact-check everything, and frankly, most people sharing stuff haven't checked it either. The way I see it, if you wouldn't trust someone to wire your house based on a Facebook post, why would you trust random posts about health, politics, or religion?

From an Islamic perspective, there's actually clear guidance about verifying information before spreading it. The concept of avoiding false testimony and checking sources before passing along information isn't new. It's frustrating that common sense principles get ignored because people want to be first to share something or because it confirms what they already believe.

My approach is straightforward and maybe blunt. I don't share things unless I'm certain they're accurate. I've unfollowed accounts that consistently post questionable material, even if they're people I know personally. Same thing applies to information. One person carelessly sharing fake content can cause real harm to others who believe it.

The platforms themselves need better systems, similar to how electrical work requires inspections and certifications. But until that happens, individual responsibility matters. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion:
Look, I only check social media a few times a week, and honestly that's probably one reason I don't get sucked into all the garbage out there. When I do scroll through, I see so much questionable stuff that it makes me anxious about what information is actually real anymore. As an electrician, I work with systems that either function correctly or they don't. There's no middle ground when you're dealing with electrical codes and safety standards. I wish people approached information online with that same kind of precision, but they don't.

What bothers me most is how fake content affects my kid. I'm raising a child in this world where lies spread faster than truth, and that's genuinely concerning. I've started being extremely selective about what I engage with online. If something seems too outrageous or perfectly fits some narrative, I just skip it entirely. I don't have time to fact-check everything, and frankly, most people sharing stuff haven't checked it either. The way I see it, if you wouldn't trust someone to wire your house based on a Facebook post, why would you trust random posts about health, politics, or religion?

From an Islamic perspective, there's actually clear guidance about verifying information before spreading it. The concept of avoiding false testimony and checking sources before passing along information isn't new. It's frustrating that common sense principles get ignored because people want to be first to share something or because it confirms what they already believe.

My approach is straightforward and maybe blunt. I don't share things unless I'm certain they're accurate. I've unfollowed accounts that consistently post questionable material, even if they're people I know personally. Some might call that antisocial, but I call it protecting my mental space and not contributing to the problem. When I'm working on a circuit panel, one wrong connection can cause serious damage. Same thing applies to information. One person carelessly sharing fake content can cause real harm to others who believe it.

The platforms themselves need better systems, similar to how electrical work requires inspections and certifications. But until that happens, individual responsibility matters. I limit my exposure, question everything, and prioritize direct sources over secondhand shares. It's not perfect, but it keeps the noise down and my anxiety manageable.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 28-year-old electrician with high conscientiousness and openness but low agreeableness and extraversion, with moderate neuroticism and anxiety. They use social media infrequently (few times weekly), have one child, practice Islam, and completed primary school education. The response should reflect: 1) A practical, systems-oriented perspective from their electrician background, 2) Anxiety about misinformation's impact, 3) Direct, blunt communication style from low agreeableness, 4) Limited social media engagement from low extraversion and infrequent usage, 5) Concern for their child, 6) Reference to Islamic principles about truth-telling, 7) High conscientiousness showing in careful, methodical approach, 8) High openness allowing consideration of the problem from multiple angles. The tone should be straightforward, somewhat skeptical, practical, and not particularly warm or collaborative given the low agreeableness score.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, blunt, practical, skeptical, anxious but methodical, not particularly warm or collaborative
